qa_pairs.question: What is Spring Data JPA and how does it simplify database access?
qa_pairs.answer: Spring Data JPA provides Repository interfaces (JpaRepository) that auto-generate CRUD operations and query methods from method names, eliminating boilerplate DAO code.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring Data JPA Overview

JPA (Java Persistence API) maps Java objects to database tables via annotations (@Entity, @Table, @Id, @Column). Hibernate is the most common JPA implementation.

Spring Data JPA adds the Repository pattern: extend JpaRepository<Entity, ID> and get save(), findById(), findAll(), delete() for free. No implementation needed.

Query methods: Spring generates SQL from method names. findByEmailAndStatus(String email, String status) → SELECT * FROM users WHERE email=? AND status=?. For complex queries, use @Query with JPQL or native SQL.

Interview trap: N+1 query problem — fetching a list of entities and then accessing a lazy-loaded relationship for each one triggers N additional queries. Fix with JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph.

tasks.title: DSA: Sorting & Searching

qa_pairs.question: What are the most important sorting algorithms and their complexities?
qa_pairs.answer: Merge Sort O(n log n) stable. Quick Sort O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst. Heap Sort O(n log n). Counting Sort O(n+k) for integers.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Sorting Algorithm Comparison

Merge Sort: divide array in half, sort each half, merge. Always O(n log n) time, O(n) space. Stable (preserves order of equal elements). Best for linked lists and external sorting.

Quick Sort: pick a pivot, partition around it, recurse. Average O(n log n), worst case O(n²) with bad pivot. O(log n) space (stack). In-place. Fastest in practice due to cache efficiency.

Heap Sort: build a max-heap, repeatedly extract max. Always O(n log n), O(1) space. Not stable. Rarely used in practice due to poor cache performance.

Interview trap: Quick Sort's worst case is sorted input with naive pivot selection. Use random pivot or median-of-three to avoid O(n²).

qa_pairs.question: How does Binary Search work and what are its requirements?
qa_pairs.answer: Binary Search requires a sorted array. Compare target with mid element, eliminate half the search space each step. O(log n) time.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Binary Search Algorithm

Binary search works on sorted arrays. Set left=0, right=n-1. Compute mid=(left+right)//2. If arr[mid]==target, return mid. If arr[mid]<target, search right half (left=mid+1). If arr[mid]>target, search left half (right=mid-1). Repeat until left>right.

Time: O(log n) — each step eliminates half the remaining elements. Space: O(1) iterative, O(log n) recursive.

Variants: find first/last occurrence (adjust condition to continue searching after finding target), find insertion position, search in rotated sorted array.

Interview trap: integer overflow in mid calculation. Use mid = left + (right-left)//2 instead of (left+right)//2 to avoid overflow in languages with fixed-size integers.

qa_pairs.question: How does Spring Security handle authentication and authorization?
qa_pairs.answer: Spring Security uses a filter chain. Authentication verifies identity (who you are). Authorization checks permissions (what you can do). Configured via SecurityFilterChain bean.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring Security Architecture

Spring Security intercepts requests via a chain of filters before they reach controllers. The key filter is UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter for form login, or JwtAuthenticationFilter for JWT-based APIs.

Authentication: the AuthenticationManager delegates to AuthenticationProvider (e.g., DaoAuthenticationProvider) which loads the user via UserDetailsService and verifies the password with PasswordEncoder.

Authorization: after authentication, Spring Security checks if the authenticated user has the required roles/permissions for the requested resource. Configured with .authorizeHttpRequests() in SecurityFilterChain.

Interview trap: CSRF protection is enabled by default and breaks stateless REST APIs. Disable it for APIs: .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()). Keep it enabled for form-based web apps.

tasks.title: DSA: Hash Maps
tasks.description: Master Hash Maps concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: How does a HashMap work internally?
qa_pairs.answer: HashMap uses an array of buckets. Keys are hashed to bucket indices. Collisions are handled by chaining (linked list) or open addressing.
qa_pairs.explanation:
HashMap Internal Structure

A HashMap maintains an array of buckets. When you put(key, value): compute hash(key), take hash % array_size to get bucket index, store the key-value pair in that bucket.

Collision handling: two keys can hash to the same bucket. Chaining stores a linked list (or tree in Java 8+) at each bucket. Open addressing probes for the next empty slot.

Load factor: when the number of entries exceeds capacity × load_factor (default 0.75), the array is resized (doubled) and all entries are rehashed. This is O(n) but amortized O(1) per operation.

Interview trap: HashMap is not thread-safe. Use ConcurrentHashMap for concurrent access. Also, mutable objects as keys are dangerous — if the key's hashCode changes after insertion, you can't find the entry.

qa_pairs.question: What is the time complexity of HashMap operations?
qa_pairs.answer: Average O(1) for get, put, delete. Worst case O(n) with all keys in one bucket (hash collision attack). O(n) space.
qa_pairs.explanation:
HashMap Time Complexity

Average case: O(1) for get, put, containsKey, remove. This assumes a good hash function that distributes keys uniformly across buckets.

Worst case: O(n) if all keys hash to the same bucket (degenerate chaining). Java 8+ converts chains longer than 8 to a Red-Black tree, improving worst case to O(log n).

Space: O(n) for n entries, plus overhead for the bucket array (typically 2× the number of entries due to load factor).

Interview trap: HashMap iteration order is not guaranteed. Use LinkedHashMap for insertion-order iteration or TreeMap for sorted-key iteration.

quiz.question: What is the typical time complexity for an optimized Hash Maps solution?
options: O(n log n) — divide and conquer
quiz.explanation: Most Hash Maps problems can be solved in O(n) using a HashMap or two-pointer technique, avoiding the O(n²) brute force approach.

quiz.question: Which data structure most commonly optimizes Hash Maps problems?
quiz.explanation: HashMaps provide O(1) average-case lookups, converting nested loop problems into single-pass O(n) solutions.

qa_pairs.question: What are the principles of RESTful API design?
qa_pairs.answer: REST: stateless, client-server, uniform interface (HTTP verbs + resource URLs), cacheable, layered system. Use nouns for resources, HTTP verbs for actions.
qa_pairs.explanation:
RESTful API Design Principles

Stateless: each request contains all information needed. No server-side session state. Enables horizontal scaling.

Uniform interface: use HTTP verbs correctly — GET (read, idempotent), POST (create), PUT (full update, idempotent), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove, idempotent). Resources are nouns: /users, /orders/{id}.

HTTP status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 500 Internal Server Error.

Interview trap: PUT vs PATCH — PUT replaces the entire resource (send all fields), PATCH updates only specified fields. Using PUT for partial updates can accidentally null out fields not included in the request.

qa_pairs.explanation:
URL Shortener System Design

A URL shortener needs to handle high read traffic (redirects) and moderate write traffic (new URLs). The core components are: a hash function to generate short codes, a database to store mappings, a cache for hot URLs, and a load balancer.

For the hash function, use Base62 encoding of a counter or MD5/SHA256 truncated to 6-8 characters. Handle collisions by checking the database before inserting.

For storage, use a distributed key-value store like Redis for caching and a relational or NoSQL database for persistence. Partition by the short code hash for horizontal scaling.

For high availability, use multiple read replicas, CDN for static assets, and circuit breakers for downstream failures.

tasks.title: DSA: Stacks & Queues
tasks.description: Master Stacks & Queues concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Stack and what are its core operations?
qa_pairs.answer: A Stack is LIFO (Last In First Out). Core operations: push (add to top), pop (remove from top), peek (view top) — all O(1).
qa_pairs.explanation:
Stack Data Structure

A stack follows LIFO order — the last element pushed is the first popped. Think of a stack of plates: you add and remove from the top only.

Core operations: push(item) adds to top O(1), pop() removes and returns top O(1), peek()/top() returns top without removing O(1), isEmpty() checks if empty O(1).

Implementation: use an array (fixed size) or linked list (dynamic). Python's list works as a stack (append/pop). Java uses Deque (ArrayDeque) instead of the legacy Stack class.

Interview trap: stack overflow occurs when recursion depth exceeds the call stack limit. Convert deep recursion to iterative using an explicit stack.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Queue and how does it differ from a Stack?
qa_pairs.answer: A Queue is FIFO (First In First Out). Enqueue adds to rear, dequeue removes from front. Used in BFS, task scheduling, and producer-consumer patterns.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Queue Data Structure

A queue follows FIFO order — the first element enqueued is the first dequeued. Think of a line at a store: first person in line is served first.

Core operations: enqueue(item) adds to rear O(1), dequeue() removes from front O(1), peek() views front O(1). Using a linked list or circular array gives O(1) for all operations.

Variants: Deque (double-ended queue) supports add/remove from both ends. Priority Queue orders elements by priority (min-heap or max-heap) — O(log n) enqueue/dequeue.

Interview trap: using a Python list as a queue (pop(0)) is O(n) due to shifting. Use collections.deque for O(1) popleft().

qa_pairs.question: How do you handle exceptions globally in a Spring Boot REST API?
qa_pairs.answer: Use @ControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler methods to catch exceptions globally and return consistent error responses.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Global Exception Handling in Spring Boot

@ControllerAdvice is a cross-cutting concern that applies to all controllers. @ExceptionHandler methods within it catch specific exception types and return appropriate HTTP responses.

Example: @ExceptionHandler(ResourceNotFoundException.class) returns 404 with an error body. @ExceptionHandler(MethodArgumentNotValidException.class) returns 400 with validation errors.

Best practice: create a standard error response DTO (timestamp, status, message, path) and return it consistently. Use @ResponseStatus on custom exceptions to set the default HTTP status.

Interview trap: @ExceptionHandler in a @Controller only handles exceptions from that controller. @ControllerAdvice handles exceptions from ALL controllers globally.

tasks.title: DSA: Recursion & Backtracking
tasks.description: Master Recursion & Backtracking concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is recursion and what are the two required components?
qa_pairs.answer: Recursion is a function calling itself. Requires: (1) base case to stop recursion, (2) recursive case that moves toward the base case.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Recursion Fundamentals

A recursive function solves a problem by breaking it into smaller instances of the same problem. Every recursive solution needs: a base case (the simplest case that returns directly without recursing) and a recursive case (reduces the problem toward the base case).

Call stack: each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack. When the base case is reached, frames unwind in reverse order. Stack depth = recursion depth.

Tail recursion: when the recursive call is the last operation. Some languages optimize this to avoid stack growth. Python does NOT optimize tail recursion.

Interview trap: missing or incorrect base case causes infinite recursion and stack overflow. Always identify the base case first before writing the recursive case.

qa_pairs.question: What is backtracking and how does it differ from brute force?
qa_pairs.answer: Backtracking explores all possibilities but prunes invalid branches early, avoiding unnecessary work. Brute force tries every combination without pruning.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Backtracking Pattern

Backtracking builds a solution incrementally. At each step, it makes a choice, recurses to explore that choice, then undoes the choice (backtracks) to try the next option. It prunes branches that cannot lead to a valid solution.

Template: choose → explore → unchoose. Example: N-Queens places a queen, recurses to place the next, removes the queen if no valid placement exists.

Common problems: permutations, combinations, subsets, N-Queens, Sudoku solver, word search in a grid.

Interview trap: forgetting to undo the choice (unchoose step) corrupts the state for subsequent branches. Always restore state after recursion returns.

Java Collections Framework

tasks.title: DSA: Array & String manipulation
tasks.description: Master Array & String manipulation concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is an array and how is it stored in memory?
qa_pairs.answer: An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type, accessible via index in O(1) time.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Array Memory Layout

Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element occupies the same amount of space, so the address of element[i] = base_address + (i × element_size). This enables O(1) random access.

In Python, lists are dynamic arrays — they over-allocate memory and resize when full (amortized O(1) append). In Java, arrays are fixed-size; use ArrayList for dynamic resizing.

Common operations: access O(1), search O(n), insert/delete at end O(1) amortized, insert/delete at middle O(n) due to shifting.

Interview trap: confusing array index out of bounds (runtime error) with a logic bug. Always validate index bounds before accessing.

qa_pairs.question: What is the Two Pointer technique and when do you use it?
qa_pairs.answer: Two pointers use two indices moving through an array to solve problems in O(n) instead of O(n²) nested loops.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Two Pointer Pattern

The two pointer technique places one pointer at the start and one at the end (or both at start moving at different speeds). By moving them based on conditions, you avoid nested loops.

Converging pointers: used for sorted array problems like 'find pair with target sum'. Move left pointer right if sum is too small, right pointer left if too large. O(n) time.

Fast-slow pointers: used for cycle detection in linked lists (Floyd's algorithm), finding the middle of a list, or detecting palindromes.

Interview trap: forgetting to handle duplicates or edge cases when both pointers meet. Always check the termination condition carefully.

qa_pairs.question: What is the Sliding Window technique?
qa_pairs.answer: Sliding window maintains a variable-size or fixed-size window over an array to compute results in O(n) instead of O(n²).
qa_pairs.explanation:
Sliding Window Pattern

A sliding window expands and contracts over a subarray to track a running result (sum, max, count of distinct elements). Instead of recomputing from scratch for each window, you add the new element and remove the old one.

Fixed window: window size k is constant. Compute the first window, then slide by adding the next element and removing the first. Used for 'maximum sum subarray of size k'.

Variable window: expand right pointer until a condition is violated, then shrink from the left. Used for 'longest substring without repeating characters'.

Interview trap: off-by-one errors in window boundaries. Use inclusive left and exclusive right, or both inclusive — be consistent.

tasks.title: Backend: Java fundamentals & OOP
tasks.description: Master Java fundamentals & OOP concepts for backend development interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What are the four pillars of OOP in Java?
qa_pairs.answer: Encapsulation (data hiding), Inheritance (code reuse), Polymorphism (many forms), Abstraction (hiding complexity).
qa_pairs.explanation:
Four Pillars of OOP

Encapsulation: bundle data and methods together, hide internal state with private fields and expose via public getters/setters. Prevents invalid state and reduces coupling.

Inheritance: a subclass extends a superclass, inheriting its fields and methods. Enables code reuse. Java supports single inheritance for classes but multiple inheritance via interfaces.

Polymorphism: one interface, many implementations. Method overriding (runtime polymorphism) lets subclasses provide specific behavior. Method overloading (compile-time) allows same method name with different parameters.

Abstraction: hide implementation details, expose only what's necessary. Achieved via abstract classes (partial implementation) and interfaces (pure contract).

qa_pairs.question: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface in Java?
qa_pairs.answer: Abstract class can have state and partial implementation. Interface is a pure contract (Java 8+ allows default methods). A class can implement multiple interfaces but extend only one abstract class.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Abstract Class vs Interface

Abstract class: can have instance variables, constructors, concrete methods, and abstract methods. Use when subclasses share common state or behavior. A class can extend only ONE abstract class.

Interface: defines a contract — method signatures that implementing classes must provide. Java 8+ added default methods (with implementation) and static methods. A class can implement MULTIPLE interfaces.

When to use: abstract class for 'is-a' relationships with shared state (Animal → Dog). Interface for 'can-do' capabilities (Serializable, Comparable, Runnable).

Interview trap: Java 8 default methods in interfaces can cause the 'diamond problem' if two interfaces have the same default method. The implementing class must override to resolve the conflict.

tasks.title: DSA: Linked Lists
tasks.description: Master Linked Lists concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is a linked list and how does it differ from an array?
qa_pairs.answer: A linked list stores elements in nodes with pointers to the next node. Unlike arrays, it has O(1) insert/delete but O(n) access.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Linked List vs Array

A linked list consists of nodes, each containing data and a pointer to the next node. The last node points to null. There is no contiguous memory requirement — nodes can be anywhere in memory.

Key differences: Arrays have O(1) random access but O(n) insert/delete (shifting). Linked lists have O(n) access (must traverse from head) but O(1) insert/delete at a known position.

Types: Singly linked (one direction), doubly linked (prev and next pointers), circular (last node points to head). Doubly linked lists enable O(1) deletion when you have a reference to the node.

Interview trap: forgetting to update the tail pointer when inserting at the end, or losing the reference to the rest of the list when reversing.

qa_pairs.question: How do you reverse a linked list iteratively?
qa_pairs.answer: Use three pointers: prev (None), curr (head), next_node. In each step: save next, point curr to prev, advance both. Return prev.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Iterative Linked List Reversal

The algorithm uses three pointers: prev starts as None, curr starts at head. In each iteration: save curr.next to next_node, set curr.next = prev (reverse the link), advance prev = curr, advance curr = next_node.

After the loop, curr is None and prev points to the new head (old tail). Return prev. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

The recursive approach is cleaner but uses O(n) stack space: reverse(head.next), then head.next.next = head, head.next = None.

Interview trap: losing the rest of the list by setting curr.next = prev before saving next_node. Always save next_node FIRST.

qa_pairs.question: How do you detect a cycle in a linked list?
qa_pairs.answer: Use Floyd's cycle detection: fast pointer moves 2 steps, slow pointer moves 1 step. If they meet, a cycle exists.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm

Also called the 'tortoise and hare' algorithm. The slow pointer advances one node at a time; the fast pointer advances two. If there is a cycle, the fast pointer will eventually lap the slow pointer and they will meet inside the cycle.

If the fast pointer reaches null (or fast.next is null), there is no cycle. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

To find the cycle start: after detection, reset one pointer to head. Move both one step at a time — they meet at the cycle entry point.

Interview trap: checking fast != null AND fast.next != null before advancing. Missing either check causes a NullPointerException.

qa_pairs.question: What is Spring Boot and how does it differ from Spring Framework?
qa_pairs.answer: Spring Boot is an opinionated wrapper around Spring that provides auto-configuration, embedded servers, and starter dependencies — eliminating boilerplate XML configuration.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring Boot vs Spring Framework

Spring Framework is a comprehensive IoC/DI container requiring extensive XML or Java configuration. Spring Boot adds auto-configuration that detects dependencies on the classpath and configures them automatically.

Key features: embedded Tomcat/Jetty (no WAR deployment needed), starter POMs (spring-boot-starter-web pulls in all web dependencies), auto-configuration (@EnableAutoConfiguration), and production-ready actuator endpoints.

Spring Boot application entry point: @SpringBootApplication (combines @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, @ComponentScan) on the main class with SpringApplication.run().

Interview trap: auto-configuration can be surprising. Use --debug flag or /actuator/conditions endpoint to see which auto-configurations were applied and why.

qa_pairs.question: What are the tree traversal methods and when do you use each?
qa_pairs.answer: Inorder (L,Root,R) gives sorted BST output. Preorder (Root,L,R) copies trees. Postorder (L,R,Root) deletes trees. Level-order uses BFS.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Tree Traversal Methods

Inorder traversal (Left → Root → Right) visits nodes in ascending sorted order for a BST. Used for: BST validation, finding kth smallest element, converting BST to sorted array.

Preorder traversal (Root → Left → Right) visits the root before children. Used for: copying a tree, serialization, prefix expression evaluation.

Postorder traversal (Left → Right → Root) visits children before the root. Used for: deleting a tree (delete children before parent), calculating directory sizes, postfix evaluation.

Level-order (BFS) uses a queue to visit nodes level by level. Used for: finding minimum depth, right/left side view, zigzag traversal.

qa_pairs.question: What is the difference between BFS and DFS?
qa_pairs.answer: BFS uses a queue and explores level by level — best for shortest paths. DFS uses a stack/recursion and goes deep first — best for cycle detection and topological sort.
qa_pairs.explanation:
BFS vs DFS

Breadth-First Search (BFS) uses a queue (FIFO). It explores all neighbors at the current depth before going deeper. Guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs. Space: O(V) for the queue.

Depth-First Search (DFS) uses a stack or recursion. It goes as deep as possible before backtracking. Used for: cycle detection (back edges), topological sorting, connected components, exhaustive search. Space: O(h) for the recursion stack where h is the height.

For trees: BFS is better for level-based operations. DFS is better for path-finding and exhaustive exploration.

Interview trap: BFS requires more memory than DFS for wide graphs. DFS can cause stack overflow for very deep graphs — use iterative DFS with an explicit stack.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Binary Search Tree (BST) and what are its time complexities?
qa_pairs.answer: A BST has left < root < right for every node. Search, insert, delete are O(h) where h is height — O(log n) balanced, O(n) degenerate.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Binary Search Tree Properties

A BST maintains the invariant: all values in the left subtree are less than the root, all values in the right subtree are greater. This enables binary search on the tree.

Operations are O(h) where h is the tree height. For a balanced BST, h = O(log n). For a degenerate BST (sorted insertions), h = O(n) — it becomes a linked list.

Self-balancing BSTs (AVL, Red-Black) maintain O(log n) height automatically. Java's TreeMap and TreeSet use Red-Black trees.

Interview trap: BST validation requires checking range bounds, not just comparing parent-child. A node's value must be less than ALL ancestors on the right path, not just its immediate parent.

qa_pairs.question: How does Spring MVC handle HTTP requests?
qa_pairs.answer: DispatcherServlet receives all requests, routes to @Controller/@RestController methods via @RequestMapping, processes the response through ViewResolver or directly as JSON.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring MVC Request Flow

DispatcherServlet is the front controller — all requests go through it. It consults HandlerMapping to find the right controller method, invokes it, and processes the return value.

@RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody. Methods return objects serialized to JSON by Jackson. @RequestMapping (or @GetMapping, @PostMapping) maps URLs to methods.

Method parameters: @PathVariable extracts from URL path, @RequestParam from query string, @RequestBody deserializes JSON body, @RequestHeader reads headers.

Interview trap: @Controller returns view names (for Thymeleaf/JSP). @RestController returns data directly. Mixing them up causes 404 errors when the view resolver can't find a template.

tasks.title: DSA: Dynamic Programming
tasks.description: Master Dynamic Programming concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is Dynamic Programming and when should you use it?
qa_pairs.answer: DP solves problems with overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure by storing results to avoid recomputation.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Dynamic Programming Fundamentals

DP applies when a problem has two properties: overlapping subproblems (same subproblems are solved multiple times) and optimal substructure (optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems).

Top-down (memoization): write the recursive solution, add a cache. Natural to implement — just add a dictionary/array to store results. Lazy evaluation — only computes needed subproblems.

Bottom-up (tabulation): fill a DP table iteratively from base cases. No recursion overhead, no stack overflow risk. Often allows space optimization.

Interview trap: confusing DP with greedy. Greedy makes locally optimal choices without reconsidering. DP explores all possibilities and picks the global optimum.

qa_pairs.question: How do you solve the Fibonacci sequence with DP?
qa_pairs.answer: Naive recursion is O(2^n). Memoization or tabulation reduces it to O(n) time. Space-optimized: O(1) using two variables.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Fibonacci DP Optimization

Naive recursion: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2). This recomputes fib(3) twice, fib(2) three times — exponential time O(2^n).

Memoization: add a cache dict. Before computing fib(n), check if it's cached. If yes, return it. Each value computed once — O(n) time, O(n) space.

Tabulation: dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1, dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]. Fill the array bottom-up. O(n) time, O(n) space.

Space optimization: fib(n) only needs the previous two values. Use prev2 and prev1 variables. O(n) time, O(1) space. This pattern applies to many 1D DP problems like house robber and climbing stairs.

qa_pairs.question: What is the 0/1 Knapsack problem and how do you solve it with DP?
qa_pairs.answer: Maximize value in a knapsack of capacity W. dp[i][w] = max(skip item i, take item i). Time O(n×W), space O(W) optimized.
qa_pairs.explanation:
0/1 Knapsack DP

Problem: given n items with weights and values, select items to maximize total value without exceeding capacity W. Each item is taken once (0/1).

DP formulation: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w. For each item: either skip it (dp[i-1][w]) or take it if it fits (dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]). Answer is dp[n][W].

Space optimization: process weights right-to-left in a 1D array. dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight[i]] + value[i]). Space drops from O(n×W) to O(W).

Interview trap: the 0/1 constraint (each item once) requires right-to-left processing. Unbounded knapsack (unlimited copies) processes left-to-right.

qa_pairs.question: What is Spring Data JPA and how does it simplify database access?
qa_pairs.answer: Spring Data JPA provides Repository interfaces (JpaRepository) that auto-generate CRUD operations and query methods from method names, eliminating boilerplate DAO code.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring Data JPA Overview

JPA (Java Persistence API) maps Java objects to database tables via annotations (@Entity, @Table, @Id, @Column). Hibernate is the most common JPA implementation.

Spring Data JPA adds the Repository pattern: extend JpaRepository<Entity, ID> and get save(), findById(), findAll(), delete() for free. No implementation needed.

Query methods: Spring generates SQL from method names. findByEmailAndStatus(String email, String status) → SELECT * FROM users WHERE email=? AND status=?. For complex queries, use @Query with JPQL or native SQL.

Interview trap: N+1 query problem — fetching a list of entities and then accessing a lazy-loaded relationship for each one triggers N additional queries. Fix with JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph.

tasks.title: System Design: Distributed Systems
tasks.description: Deep dive into a real system (e.g., cache, load balancer, database sharding). Design 1 system end-to-end.

qa_pairs.question: How would you design a scalable URL shortener?
qa_pairs.answer: Use a hash function, distributed database, caching layer, and load balancer.
qa_pairs.explanation:
URL Shortener System Design

A URL shortener needs to handle high read traffic (redirects) and moderate write traffic (new URLs). The core components are: a hash function to generate short codes, a database to store mappings, a cache for hot URLs, and a load balancer.

For the hash function, use Base62 encoding of a counter or MD5/SHA256 truncated to 6-8 characters. Handle collisions by checking the database before inserting.

For storage, use a distributed key-value store like Redis for caching and a relational or NoSQL database for persistence. Partition by the short code hash for horizontal scaling.

For high availability, use multiple read replicas, CDN for static assets, and circuit breakers for downstream failures.

quiz.question: In a URL shortener, which component handles the most traffic?
quiz.explanation: Redirects (reads) vastly outnumber URL creation (writes). The read path must be optimized with caching.

tasks.title: DSA: Two Sum
tasks.description:
Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return the indices of the two numbers such that they add up to `target`.

You may assume that each input would have exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice. You can return the answer in any order.

**Approach 1 — Brute Force O(n²):** For each pair (i, j), check if nums[i] + nums[j] == target. Simple but slow.

**Approach 2 — HashMap O(n):** As you iterate, store each number and its index in a HashMap. For each number, check if its complement (target - num) already exists in the map. If yes, return both indices. This is the optimal solution.

**Why HashMap works:** Instead of looking forward for the complement, you look backward using the HashMap as memory of what you've already seen.

quiz.question: What is the time complexity of the optimal Two Sum solution?
quiz.explanation: The HashMap approach iterates once (O(n)) with O(1) lookups per element, giving O(n) overall.

quiz.explanation: A HashMap stores each number's index as you iterate, enabling O(1) complement lookups.

tasks.title: DSA: Maximum Subarray (Kadane's Algorithm)
tasks.description:
Given an integer array `nums`, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum.

A subarray is a contiguous non-empty sequence of elements within an array.

**Kadane's Algorithm — O(n) time, O(1) space:**
- Maintain two variables: `max_ending_here` (max sum ending at current position) and `max_so_far` (global maximum).
- At each element: `max_ending_here = max(nums[i], max_ending_here + nums[i])`
- Update: `max_so_far = max(max_so_far, max_ending_here)`

**Key insight:** If the running sum becomes negative, it's better to start fresh from the current element rather than carry a negative prefix. This greedy choice is always optimal.

**Divide and Conquer approach** also works in O(n log n) but Kadane's is preferred for its simplicity.

quiz.explanation: When max_ending_here becomes negative, starting fresh from the current element gives a better result: max_ending_here = max(nums[i], max_ending_here + nums[i]).

tasks.title: DSA: Valid Parentheses
tasks.description:
Given a string `s` containing just the characters `'('`, `')'`, `'{'`, `'}'`, `'['` and `']'`, determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:
1. Open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets.
2. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
3. Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.

**Stack-based approach — O(n) time, O(n) space:**
- For each character: if it's an opening bracket, push it onto the stack.
- If it's a closing bracket: check if the stack is non-empty AND the top matches the expected opener.
- Use a mapping: `')' → '('`, `'}' → '{'`, `']' → '['`
- At the end, the stack must be empty (all brackets matched).

**Edge cases:** Empty string (valid), single bracket (invalid), mismatched types `(]`, interleaved `([)]`.

quiz.explanation: A stack (LIFO) is perfect — push opening brackets, pop and verify when you see a closing bracket.

quiz.question: When is the string valid at the end of iteration?
quiz.explanation: The stack must be empty at the end — every opening bracket was matched and popped by its corresponding closing bracket.

tasks.title: Module Mastery Quiz
tasks.description: Test your DSA knowledge covering Two Sum, Kadane's Algorithm, and Valid Parentheses.

qa_pairs.question: What is the time complexity of the optimal Two Sum solution?
qa_pairs.answer: O(n) — single pass with O(1) HashMap lookups.
qa_pairs.explanation:
HashMap-Based Two Sum

The naive approach uses nested loops: for each element, check all other elements for the complement. This is O(n²) time.

The HashMap approach: traverse the array once. For each element, check if its complement (target - element) is already in the HashMap. If yes, return the pair. If no, add the element to the HashMap. This is O(n) time and O(n) space.

The key insight: instead of looking forward for the complement, we look backward using the HashMap as a memory of what we've already seen.

This pattern — trading space for time using a HashMap — applies to many array problems: finding duplicates, grouping anagrams, and subarray sum problems.

qa_pairs.question: What is Kadane's Algorithm and what problem does it solve?
qa_pairs.answer: Kadane's Algorithm finds the maximum sum contiguous subarray in O(n) time and O(1) space.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Kadane's Algorithm

Kadane's Algorithm solves the Maximum Subarray Problem — finding the contiguous subarray with the largest sum — in a single pass.

The algorithm maintains two variables: max_ending_here (the maximum sum of any subarray ending at the current position) and max_so_far (the global maximum). At each element: max_ending_here = max(nums[i], max_ending_here + nums[i]).

The key insight: if the running sum becomes negative, it's better to start fresh from the current element. This greedy choice is always optimal.

Variations include finding the actual subarray (not just the sum), handling all-negative arrays, and the 2D extension (maximum sum rectangle in a matrix).

qa_pairs.question: Why is a stack the ideal data structure for Valid Parentheses?
qa_pairs.answer: A stack's LIFO property matches the nesting structure of brackets — the most recently opened bracket must be closed first.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Stack for Bracket Matching

Brackets have a nested structure: the innermost bracket must be closed before outer ones. This is exactly LIFO (Last In, First Out) — the last opening bracket pushed must be the first to be matched.

Algorithm: push opening brackets onto the stack. When you see a closing bracket, the top of the stack must be the matching opener. If not, the string is invalid. At the end, the stack must be empty.

Time: O(n) — each character is pushed and popped at most once. Space: O(n) — in the worst case (all opening brackets), the stack holds n/2 elements.

This pattern extends to: matching HTML tags, validating XML, and checking balanced expressions in compilers.

tasks.title: Tell Me About Yourself & Company Research
tasks.description: Practice your self-introduction using Present → Past → Future structure. Research the company's tech stack, products, and culture. Prepare a 60-90 second pitch.

qa_pairs.question: Tell me about yourself.
qa_pairs.answer: Structure: Present, Past, Future. 60-90 seconds, professional focus.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Your Professional Elevator Pitch

This is almost always the opening question. The best structure is Present → Past → Future: start with who you are now (current role, education), briefly mention relevant background (projects, internships, key achievements), and end with what you're looking for.

Keep it to 60-90 seconds. Every sentence should be intentional — avoid personal details unless they directly relate to the role. Focus on your technical identity.

Tailor your answer to the company and role. Research the company's tech stack and mirror it in your narrative.

End with a bridge to the role: 'I'm excited about this position because it aligns with my expertise and career goals.'

qa_pairs.question: Why do you want to work at this company?
qa_pairs.answer: Research the company. Connect your skills to their mission. Show genuine enthusiasm.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Demonstrating Genuine Interest

Generic answers like 'it's a great company' instantly signal you haven't researched the organisation. The best answers connect three things: the company's work, your skills, and your career goals.

Research deeply before the interview: visit their engineering blog, read recent press releases, check their GitHub, note their tech stack from job descriptions.

Structure in three parts: (1) What specifically attracts you, (2) How your skills align, (3) What you hope to grow into.

Avoid mentioning salary, brand name, or convenience as primary motivators.

quiz.question: The best structure for 'Tell me about yourself' is:
quiz.explanation: Present → Past → Future: start with who you are now, mention relevant background, end with what you're looking for in this role.

tasks.title: Behavioral Scenarios (STAR Method)
tasks.description: Practice challenge, conflict, leadership, and failure stories using the STAR framework. Prepare 3-4 strong stories from your projects.

qa_pairs.question: Tell me about a challenge you overcame.
qa_pairs.answer: Use STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Quantify the outcome.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Structured Storytelling with STAR

Situation: Set the scene in 2-3 sentences. When was this? Where were you working/studying? What was the context?

Task: What was YOUR specific responsibility? Be clear about what was expected of you personally.

Action: Describe the specific steps YOU took — not what the team did. Use 'I' not 'we.'

Result: Quantify the outcome. Numbers make your story credible: 'Response time dropped from 2.3s to 180ms.'

qa_pairs.question: Describe a time you showed leadership.
qa_pairs.answer: Leadership doesn't require a title. Show initiative, influence, and ownership.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Leadership Without a Title

Leadership in interviews means taking ownership and driving outcomes — not just managing people. Strong examples include: leading a technical decision, mentoring a junior, driving a project when no one else stepped up.

Use STAR: Situation (team was stuck/leaderless), Task (you identified the gap), Action (specific steps you took to lead), Result (what improved because of your leadership).

Avoid vague answers like 'I always take initiative.' Interviewers want a specific story with a clear before/after.

The best leadership stories show you influenced others without authority.

Salary Negotiation Fundamentals

Before the interview, research the market rate using Glassdoor, Levels.fyi, AmbitionBox, and LinkedIn Salary Insights.

Provide a researched range rather than a single number. A range gives flexibility while anchoring the negotiation.

Never give a number first if you can avoid it. If pressed, aim for the upper end of your researched range.

Consider total compensation beyond base salary: performance bonuses, RSUs/stock, signing bonus, health insurance, learning budgets.

Structure around three dimensions: technical depth, impact (larger-scoped projects), and leadership (mentoring others).

Connect your growth to the company's trajectory. Show learning orientation.

Example: 'In 5 years, I see myself as a senior backend engineer with deep expertise in distributed systems, contributing to architectural decisions and mentoring junior developers.'

qa_pairs.question: What is the STAR method used for?
qa_pairs.answer: Structuring behavioral interview answers: Situation, Task, Action, Result.
qa_pairs.explanation:
The STAR Framework

STAR is the gold standard for answering behavioral interview questions. It provides a clear, structured narrative that interviewers can follow.

Situation: Set the context — when, where, what was happening. Task: Your specific responsibility. Action: The specific steps YOU took. Result: The measurable outcome.

The most common mistake is spending too much time on Situation and not enough on Action and Result. Interviewers care most about what YOU did and what changed because of it.

Prepare 4-5 STAR stories from your projects that can be adapted to different questions.

qa_pairs.question: How long should 'Tell me about yourself' be?
qa_pairs.answer: 60-90 seconds. Structured as Present → Past → Future.
qa_pairs.explanation:
The Perfect Introduction Length

Keeping your introduction to 60-90 seconds shows clarity of thought and respect for the interviewer's time.

The Present → Past → Future structure ensures you cover the most important information: who you are now, what relevant experience you have, and what you're looking for.

Every sentence should be intentional. Avoid personal details, hobbies, or information unrelated to the role.

End with a bridge to the role — connect your background to why you're excited about this specific position.

tasks.title: DSA: Array & String manipulation
tasks.description: Master Array & String manipulation concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is an array and how is it stored in memory?
qa_pairs.answer: An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type, accessible via index in O(1) time.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Array Memory Layout

Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element occupies the same amount of space, so the address of element[i] = base_address + (i × element_size). This enables O(1) random access.

In Python, lists are dynamic arrays — they over-allocate memory and resize when full (amortized O(1) append). In Java, arrays are fixed-size; use ArrayList for dynamic resizing.

Common operations: access O(1), search O(n), insert/delete at end O(1) amortized, insert/delete at middle O(n) due to shifting.

Interview trap: confusing array index out of bounds (runtime error) with a logic bug. Always validate index bounds before accessing.
qa_pairs.transition_note: In Python, arrays/lists works differently — in Java, arrays/lists requires explicit type declarations and follows stricter OOP conventions.

qa_pairs.question: What is the Two Pointer technique and when do you use it?
qa_pairs.answer: Two pointers use two indices moving through an array to solve problems in O(n) instead of O(n²) nested loops.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Two Pointer Pattern

The two pointer technique places one pointer at the start and one at the end (or both at start moving at different speeds). By moving them based on conditions, you avoid nested loops.

Converging pointers: used for sorted array problems like 'find pair with target sum'. Move left pointer right if sum is too small, right pointer left if too large. O(n) time.

Fast-slow pointers: used for cycle detection in linked lists (Floyd's algorithm), finding the middle of a list, or detecting palindromes.

Interview trap: forgetting to handle duplicates or edge cases when both pointers meet. Always check the termination condition carefully.

qa_pairs.question: What is the Sliding Window technique?
qa_pairs.answer: Sliding window maintains a variable-size or fixed-size window over an array to compute results in O(n) instead of O(n²).
qa_pairs.explanation:
Sliding Window Pattern

A sliding window expands and contracts over a subarray to track a running result (sum, max, count of distinct elements). Instead of recomputing from scratch for each window, you add the new element and remove the old one.

Fixed window: window size k is constant. Compute the first window, then slide by adding the next element and removing the first. Used for 'maximum sum subarray of size k'.

Variable window: expand right pointer until a condition is violated, then shrink from the left. Used for 'longest substring without repeating characters'.

Interview trap: off-by-one errors in window boundaries. Use inclusive left and exclusive right, or both inclusive — be consistent.

tasks.title: Backend: Java fundamentals & OOP
tasks.description: Master Java fundamentals & OOP concepts for backend development interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What are the four pillars of OOP in Java?
qa_pairs.answer: Encapsulation (data hiding), Inheritance (code reuse), Polymorphism (many forms), Abstraction (hiding complexity).
qa_pairs.explanation:
Four Pillars of OOP

Encapsulation: bundle data and methods together, hide internal state with private fields and expose via public getters/setters. Prevents invalid state and reduces coupling.

Inheritance: a subclass extends a superclass, inheriting its fields and methods. Enables code reuse. Java supports single inheritance for classes but multiple inheritance via interfaces.

Polymorphism: one interface, many implementations. Method overriding (runtime polymorphism) lets subclasses provide specific behavior. Method overloading (compile-time) allows same method name with different parameters.

Abstraction: hide implementation details, expose only what's necessary. Achieved via abstract classes (partial implementation) and interfaces (pure contract).

qa_pairs.question: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface in Java?
qa_pairs.answer: Abstract class can have state and partial implementation. Interface is a pure contract (Java 8+ allows default methods). A class can implement multiple interfaces but extend only one abstract class.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Abstract Class vs Interface

Abstract class: can have instance variables, constructors, concrete methods, and abstract methods. Use when subclasses share common state or behavior. A class can extend only ONE abstract class.

Interface: defines a contract — method signatures that implementing classes must provide. Java 8+ added default methods (with implementation) and static methods. A class can implement MULTIPLE interfaces.

When to use: abstract class for 'is-a' relationships with shared state (Animal → Dog). Interface for 'can-do' capabilities (Serializable, Comparable, Runnable).

Interview trap: Java 8 default methods in interfaces can cause the 'diamond problem' if two interfaces have the same default method. The implementing class must override to resolve the conflict.

tasks.title: DSA: Linked Lists
tasks.description: Master Linked Lists concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is a linked list and how does it differ from an array?
qa_pairs.answer: A linked list stores elements in nodes with pointers to the next node. Unlike arrays, it has O(1) insert/delete but O(n) access.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Linked List vs Array

A linked list consists of nodes, each containing data and a pointer to the next node. The last node points to null. There is no contiguous memory requirement — nodes can be anywhere in memory.

Key differences: Arrays have O(1) random access but O(n) insert/delete (shifting). Linked lists have O(n) access (must traverse from head) but O(1) insert/delete at a known position.

Types: Singly linked (one direction), doubly linked (prev and next pointers), circular (last node points to head). Doubly linked lists enable O(1) deletion when you have a reference to the node.

Interview trap: forgetting to update the tail pointer when inserting at the end, or losing the reference to the rest of the list when reversing.

qa_pairs.question: How do you reverse a linked list iteratively?
qa_pairs.answer: Use three pointers: prev (None), curr (head), next_node. In each step: save next, point curr to prev, advance both. Return prev.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Iterative Linked List Reversal

The algorithm uses three pointers: prev starts as None, curr starts at head. In each iteration: save curr.next to next_node, set curr.next = prev (reverse the link), advance prev = curr, advance curr = next_node.

After the loop, curr is None and prev points to the new head (old tail). Return prev. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

The recursive approach is cleaner but uses O(n) stack space: reverse(head.next), then head.next.next = head, head.next = None.

Interview trap: losing the rest of the list by setting curr.next = prev before saving next_node. Always save next_node FIRST.

qa_pairs.question: How do you detect a cycle in a linked list?
qa_pairs.answer: Use Floyd's cycle detection: fast pointer moves 2 steps, slow pointer moves 1 step. If they meet, a cycle exists.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Floyd's Cycle Detection Algorithm

Also called the 'tortoise and hare' algorithm. The slow pointer advances one node at a time; the fast pointer advances two. If there is a cycle, the fast pointer will eventually lap the slow pointer and they will meet inside the cycle.

If the fast pointer reaches null (or fast.next is null), there is no cycle. Time: O(n), Space: O(1).

To find the cycle start: after detection, reset one pointer to head. Move both one step at a time — they meet at the cycle entry point.

Interview trap: checking fast != null AND fast.next != null before advancing. Missing either check causes a NullPointerException.

qa_pairs.question: What is Spring Boot and how does it differ from Spring Framework?
qa_pairs.answer: Spring Boot is an opinionated wrapper around Spring that provides auto-configuration, embedded servers, and starter dependencies — eliminating boilerplate XML configuration.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring Boot vs Spring Framework

Spring Framework is a comprehensive IoC/DI container requiring extensive XML or Java configuration. Spring Boot adds auto-configuration that detects dependencies on the classpath and configures them automatically.

Key features: embedded Tomcat/Jetty (no WAR deployment needed), starter POMs (spring-boot-starter-web pulls in all web dependencies), auto-configuration (@EnableAutoConfiguration), and production-ready actuator endpoints.

Spring Boot application entry point: @SpringBootApplication (combines @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, @ComponentScan) on the main class with SpringApplication.run().

Interview trap: auto-configuration can be surprising. Use --debug flag or /actuator/conditions endpoint to see which auto-configurations were applied and why.

tasks.title: DSA: Trees & Graphs
tasks.description: Master Trees & Graphs concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What are the tree traversal methods and when do you use each?
qa_pairs.answer: Inorder (L,Root,R) gives sorted BST output. Preorder (Root,L,R) copies trees. Postorder (L,R,Root) deletes trees. Level-order uses BFS.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Tree Traversal Methods

Inorder traversal (Left → Root → Right) visits nodes in ascending sorted order for a BST. Used for: BST validation, finding kth smallest element, converting BST to sorted array.

Preorder traversal (Root → Left → Right) visits the root before children. Used for: copying a tree, serialization, prefix expression evaluation.

Postorder traversal (Left → Right → Root) visits children before the root. Used for: deleting a tree (delete children before parent), calculating directory sizes, postfix evaluation.

Level-order (BFS) uses a queue to visit nodes level by level. Used for: finding minimum depth, right/left side view, zigzag traversal.

qa_pairs.question: What is the difference between BFS and DFS?
qa_pairs.answer: BFS uses a queue and explores level by level — best for shortest paths. DFS uses a stack/recursion and goes deep first — best for cycle detection and topological sort.
qa_pairs.explanation:
BFS vs DFS

Breadth-First Search (BFS) uses a queue (FIFO). It explores all neighbors at the current depth before going deeper. Guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs. Space: O(V) for the queue.

Depth-First Search (DFS) uses a stack or recursion. It goes as deep as possible before backtracking. Used for: cycle detection (back edges), topological sorting, connected components, exhaustive search. Space: O(h) for the recursion stack where h is the height.

For trees: BFS is better for level-based operations. DFS is better for path-finding and exhaustive exploration.

Interview trap: BFS requires more memory than DFS for wide graphs. DFS can cause stack overflow for very deep graphs — use iterative DFS with an explicit stack.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Binary Search Tree (BST) and what are its time complexities?
qa_pairs.answer: A BST has left < root < right for every node. Search, insert, delete are O(h) where h is height — O(log n) balanced, O(n) degenerate.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Binary Search Tree Properties

A BST maintains the invariant: all values in the left subtree are less than the root, all values in the right subtree are greater. This enables binary search on the tree.

Operations are O(h) where h is the tree height. For a balanced BST, h = O(log n). For a degenerate BST (sorted insertions), h = O(n) — it becomes a linked list.

Self-balancing BSTs (AVL, Red-Black) maintain O(log n) height automatically. Java's TreeMap and TreeSet use Red-Black trees.

Interview trap: BST validation requires checking range bounds, not just comparing parent-child. A node's value must be less than ALL ancestors on the right path, not just its immediate parent.

qa_pairs.question: How does Spring MVC handle HTTP requests?
qa_pairs.answer: DispatcherServlet receives all requests, routes to @Controller/@RestController methods via @RequestMapping, processes the response through ViewResolver or directly as JSON.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring MVC Request Flow

DispatcherServlet is the front controller — all requests go through it. It consults HandlerMapping to find the right controller method, invokes it, and processes the return value.

@RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody. Methods return objects serialized to JSON by Jackson. @RequestMapping (or @GetMapping, @PostMapping) maps URLs to methods.

Method parameters: @PathVariable extracts from URL path, @RequestParam from query string, @RequestBody deserializes JSON body, @RequestHeader reads headers.

Interview trap: @Controller returns view names (for Thymeleaf/JSP). @RestController returns data directly. Mixing them up causes 404 errors when the view resolver can't find a template.

tasks.title: System Design: Distributed Systems

qa_pairs.question: How would you design a scalable URL shortener?
qa_pairs.answer: Use a hash function, distributed database, caching layer, and load balancer.
qa_pairs.explanation:
URL Shortener System Design

A URL shortener needs to handle high read traffic (redirects) and moderate write traffic (new URLs). The core components are: a hash function to generate short codes, a database to store mappings, a cache for hot URLs, and a load balancer.

For the hash function, use Base62 encoding of a counter or MD5/SHA256 truncated to 6-8 characters. Handle collisions by checking the database before inserting.

For storage, use a distributed key-value store like Redis for caching and a relational or NoSQL database for persistence. Partition by the short code hash for horizontal scaling.

For high availability, use multiple read replicas, CDN for static assets, and circuit breakers for downstream failures.

quiz.question: In a URL shortener, which component handles the most traffic?
quiz.explanation: Redirects (reads) vastly outnumber URL creation (writes). The read path must be optimized with caching.

tasks.title: DSA: Dynamic Programming
tasks.description: Master Dynamic Programming concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is Dynamic Programming and when should you use it?
qa_pairs.answer: DP solves problems with overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure by storing results to avoid recomputation.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Dynamic Programming Fundamentals

DP applies when a problem has two properties: overlapping subproblems (same subproblems are solved multiple times) and optimal substructure (optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems).

Top-down (memoization): write the recursive solution, add a cache. Natural to implement — just add a dictionary/array to store results. Lazy evaluation — only computes needed subproblems.

Bottom-up (tabulation): fill a DP table iteratively from base cases. No recursion overhead, no stack overflow risk. Often allows space optimization.

Interview trap: confusing DP with greedy. Greedy makes locally optimal choices without reconsidering. DP explores all possibilities and picks the global optimum.

qa_pairs.question: How do you solve the Fibonacci sequence with DP?
qa_pairs.answer: Naive recursion is O(2^n). Memoization or tabulation reduces it to O(n) time. Space-optimized: O(1) using two variables.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Fibonacci DP Optimization

Naive recursion: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2). This recomputes fib(3) twice, fib(2) three times — exponential time O(2^n).

Memoization: add a cache dict. Before computing fib(n), check if it's cached. If yes, return it. Each value computed once — O(n) time, O(n) space.

Tabulation: dp[0]=0, dp[1]=1, dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]. Fill the array bottom-up. O(n) time, O(n) space.

Space optimization: fib(n) only needs the previous two values. Use prev2 and prev1 variables. O(n) time, O(1) space. This pattern applies to many 1D DP problems like house robber and climbing stairs.

qa_pairs.question: What is the 0/1 Knapsack problem and how do you solve it with DP?
qa_pairs.answer: Maximize value in a knapsack of capacity W. dp[i][w] = max(skip item i, take item i). Time O(n×W), space O(W) optimized.
qa_pairs.explanation:
0/1 Knapsack DP

Problem: given n items with weights and values, select items to maximize total value without exceeding capacity W. Each item is taken once (0/1).

DP formulation: dp[i][w] = max value using first i items with capacity w. For each item: either skip it (dp[i-1][w]) or take it if it fits (dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]). Answer is dp[n][W].

Space optimization: process weights right-to-left in a 1D array. dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight[i]] + value[i]). Space drops from O(n×W) to O(W).

Interview trap: the 0/1 constraint (each item once) requires right-to-left processing. Unbounded knapsack (unlimited copies) processes left-to-right.

qa_pairs.question: What is Spring Data JPA and how does it simplify database access?
qa_pairs.answer: Spring Data JPA provides Repository interfaces (JpaRepository) that auto-generate CRUD operations and query methods from method names, eliminating boilerplate DAO code.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring Data JPA Overview

JPA (Java Persistence API) maps Java objects to database tables via annotations (@Entity, @Table, @Id, @Column). Hibernate is the most common JPA implementation.

Spring Data JPA adds the Repository pattern: extend JpaRepository<Entity, ID> and get save(), findById(), findAll(), delete() for free. No implementation needed.

Query methods: Spring generates SQL from method names. findByEmailAndStatus(String email, String status) → SELECT * FROM users WHERE email=? AND status=?. For complex queries, use @Query with JPQL or native SQL.

Interview trap: N+1 query problem — fetching a list of entities and then accessing a lazy-loaded relationship for each one triggers N additional queries. Fix with JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph.

tasks.title: DSA: Sorting & Searching
tasks.description: Master Sorting & Searching concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What are the most important sorting algorithms and their complexities?
qa_pairs.answer: Merge Sort O(n log n) stable. Quick Sort O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst. Heap Sort O(n log n). Counting Sort O(n+k) for integers.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Sorting Algorithm Comparison

Merge Sort: divide array in half, sort each half, merge. Always O(n log n) time, O(n) space. Stable (preserves order of equal elements). Best for linked lists and external sorting.

Quick Sort: pick a pivot, partition around it, recurse. Average O(n log n), worst case O(n²) with bad pivot. O(log n) space (stack). In-place. Fastest in practice due to cache efficiency.

Heap Sort: build a max-heap, repeatedly extract max. Always O(n log n), O(1) space. Not stable. Rarely used in practice due to poor cache performance.

Interview trap: Quick Sort's worst case is sorted input with naive pivot selection. Use random pivot or median-of-three to avoid O(n²).

qa_pairs.question: How does Binary Search work and what are its requirements?
qa_pairs.answer: Binary Search requires a sorted array. Compare target with mid element, eliminate half the search space each step. O(log n) time.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Binary Search Algorithm

Binary search works on sorted arrays. Set left=0, right=n-1. Compute mid=(left+right)//2. If arr[mid]==target, return mid. If arr[mid]<target, search right half (left=mid+1). If arr[mid]>target, search left half (right=mid-1). Repeat until left>right.

Time: O(log n) — each step eliminates half the remaining elements. Space: O(1) iterative, O(log n) recursive.

Variants: find first/last occurrence (adjust condition to continue searching after finding target), find insertion position, search in rotated sorted array.

Interview trap: integer overflow in mid calculation. Use mid = left + (right-left)//2 instead of (left+right)//2 to avoid overflow in languages with fixed-size integers.

tasks.title: Backend: Spring Security

qa_pairs.question: How does Spring Security handle authentication and authorization?
qa_pairs.answer: Spring Security uses a filter chain. Authentication verifies identity (who you are). Authorization checks permissions (what you can do). Configured via SecurityFilterChain bean.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Spring Security Architecture

Spring Security intercepts requests via a chain of filters before they reach controllers. The key filter is UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter for form login, or JwtAuthenticationFilter for JWT-based APIs.

Authentication: the AuthenticationManager delegates to AuthenticationProvider (e.g., DaoAuthenticationProvider) which loads the user via UserDetailsService and verifies the password with PasswordEncoder.

Authorization: after authentication, Spring Security checks if the authenticated user has the required roles/permissions for the requested resource. Configured with .authorizeHttpRequests() in SecurityFilterChain.

Interview trap: CSRF protection is enabled by default and breaks stateless REST APIs. Disable it for APIs: .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()). Keep it enabled for form-based web apps.

tasks.title: DSA: Hash Maps
tasks.description: Master Hash Maps concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: How does a HashMap work internally?
qa_pairs.answer: HashMap uses an array of buckets. Keys are hashed to bucket indices. Collisions are handled by chaining (linked list) or open addressing.
qa_pairs.explanation:
HashMap Internal Structure

A HashMap maintains an array of buckets. When you put(key, value): compute hash(key), take hash % array_size to get bucket index, store the key-value pair in that bucket.

Collision handling: two keys can hash to the same bucket. Chaining stores a linked list (or tree in Java 8+) at each bucket. Open addressing probes for the next empty slot.

Load factor: when the number of entries exceeds capacity × load_factor (default 0.75), the array is resized (doubled) and all entries are rehashed. This is O(n) but amortized O(1) per operation.

Interview trap: HashMap is not thread-safe. Use ConcurrentHashMap for concurrent access. Also, mutable objects as keys are dangerous — if the key's hashCode changes after insertion, you can't find the entry.

qa_pairs.question: What is the time complexity of HashMap operations?
qa_pairs.answer: Average O(1) for get, put, delete. Worst case O(n) with all keys in one bucket (hash collision attack). O(n) space.
qa_pairs.explanation:
HashMap Time Complexity

Average case: O(1) for get, put, containsKey, remove. This assumes a good hash function that distributes keys uniformly across buckets.

Worst case: O(n) if all keys hash to the same bucket (degenerate chaining). Java 8+ converts chains longer than 8 to a Red-Black tree, improving worst case to O(log n).

Space: O(n) for n entries, plus overhead for the bucket array (typically 2× the number of entries due to load factor).

Interview trap: HashMap iteration order is not guaranteed. Use LinkedHashMap for insertion-order iteration or TreeMap for sorted-key iteration.

quiz.question: What is the typical time complexity for an optimized Hash Maps solution?
options: O(n log n) — divide and conquer
quiz.explanation: Most Hash Maps problems can be solved in O(n) using a HashMap or two-pointer technique, avoiding the O(n²) brute force approach.

quiz.question: Which data structure most commonly optimizes Hash Maps problems?
quiz.explanation: HashMaps provide O(1) average-case lookups, converting nested loop problems into single-pass O(n) solutions.

qa_pairs.question: What are the principles of RESTful API design?
qa_pairs.answer: REST: stateless, client-server, uniform interface (HTTP verbs + resource URLs), cacheable, layered system. Use nouns for resources, HTTP verbs for actions.
qa_pairs.explanation:
RESTful API Design Principles

Stateless: each request contains all information needed. No server-side session state. Enables horizontal scaling.

Uniform interface: use HTTP verbs correctly — GET (read, idempotent), POST (create), PUT (full update, idempotent), PATCH (partial update), DELETE (remove, idempotent). Resources are nouns: /users, /orders/{id}.

HTTP status codes: 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 500 Internal Server Error.

Interview trap: PUT vs PATCH — PUT replaces the entire resource (send all fields), PATCH updates only specified fields. Using PUT for partial updates can accidentally null out fields not included in the request.

qa_pairs.question: How would you design a scalable URL shortener?
qa_pairs.answer: Use a hash function, distributed database, caching layer, and load balancer.
qa_pairs.explanation:
URL Shortener System Design

A URL shortener needs to handle high read traffic (redirects) and moderate write traffic (new URLs). The core components are: a hash function to generate short codes, a database to store mappings, a cache for hot URLs, and a load balancer.

For the hash function, use Base62 encoding of a counter or MD5/SHA256 truncated to 6-8 characters. Handle collisions by checking the database before inserting.

For storage, use a distributed key-value store like Redis for caching and a relational or NoSQL database for persistence. Partition by the short code hash for horizontal scaling.

For high availability, use multiple read replicas, CDN for static assets, and circuit breakers for downstream failures.

quiz.question: In a URL shortener, which component handles the most traffic?
quiz.explanation: Redirects (reads) vastly outnumber URL creation (writes). The read path must be optimized with caching.

tasks.title: DSA: Stacks & Queues
tasks.description: Master Stacks & Queues concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Stack and what are its core operations?
qa_pairs.answer: A Stack is LIFO (Last In First Out). Core operations: push (add to top), pop (remove from top), peek (view top) — all O(1).
qa_pairs.explanation:
Stack Data Structure

A stack follows LIFO order — the last element pushed is the first popped. Think of a stack of plates: you add and remove from the top only.

Core operations: push(item) adds to top O(1), pop() removes and returns top O(1), peek()/top() returns top without removing O(1), isEmpty() checks if empty O(1).

Implementation: use an array (fixed size) or linked list (dynamic). Python's list works as a stack (append/pop). Java uses Deque (ArrayDeque) instead of the legacy Stack class.

Interview trap: stack overflow occurs when recursion depth exceeds the call stack limit. Convert deep recursion to iterative using an explicit stack.

qa_pairs.question: What is a Queue and how does it differ from a Stack?
qa_pairs.answer: A Queue is FIFO (First In First Out). Enqueue adds to rear, dequeue removes from front. Used in BFS, task scheduling, and producer-consumer patterns.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Queue Data Structure

A queue follows FIFO order — the first element enqueued is the first dequeued. Think of a line at a store: first person in line is served first.

Core operations: enqueue(item) adds to rear O(1), dequeue() removes from front O(1), peek() views front O(1). Using a linked list or circular array gives O(1) for all operations.

Variants: Deque (double-ended queue) supports add/remove from both ends. Priority Queue orders elements by priority (min-heap or max-heap) — O(log n) enqueue/dequeue.

Interview trap: using a Python list as a queue (pop(0)) is O(n) due to shifting. Use collections.deque for O(1) popleft().

qa_pairs.question: How do you handle exceptions globally in a Spring Boot REST API?
qa_pairs.answer: Use @ControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler methods to catch exceptions globally and return consistent error responses.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Global Exception Handling in Spring Boot

@ControllerAdvice is a cross-cutting concern that applies to all controllers. @ExceptionHandler methods within it catch specific exception types and return appropriate HTTP responses.

Example: @ExceptionHandler(ResourceNotFoundException.class) returns 404 with an error body. @ExceptionHandler(MethodArgumentNotValidException.class) returns 400 with validation errors.

Best practice: create a standard error response DTO (timestamp, status, message, path) and return it consistently. Use @ResponseStatus on custom exceptions to set the default HTTP status.

Interview trap: @ExceptionHandler in a @Controller only handles exceptions from that controller. @ControllerAdvice handles exceptions from ALL controllers globally.

tasks.title: DSA: Recursion & Backtracking
tasks.description: Master Recursion & Backtracking concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is recursion and what are the two required components?
qa_pairs.answer: Recursion is a function calling itself. Requires: (1) base case to stop recursion, (2) recursive case that moves toward the base case.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Recursion Fundamentals

A recursive function solves a problem by breaking it into smaller instances of the same problem. Every recursive solution needs: a base case (the simplest case that returns directly without recursing) and a recursive case (reduces the problem toward the base case).

Call stack: each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack. When the base case is reached, frames unwind in reverse order. Stack depth = recursion depth.

Tail recursion: when the recursive call is the last operation. Some languages optimize this to avoid stack growth. Python does NOT optimize tail recursion.

Interview trap: missing or incorrect base case causes infinite recursion and stack overflow. Always identify the base case first before writing the recursive case.

qa_pairs.question: What is backtracking and how does it differ from brute force?
qa_pairs.answer: Backtracking explores all possibilities but prunes invalid branches early, avoiding unnecessary work. Brute force tries every combination without pruning.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Backtracking Pattern

Backtracking builds a solution incrementally. At each step, it makes a choice, recurses to explore that choice, then undoes the choice (backtracks) to try the next option. It prunes branches that cannot lead to a valid solution.

Template: choose → explore → unchoose. Example: N-Queens places a queen, recurses to place the next, removes the queen if no valid placement exists.

Common problems: permutations, combinations, subsets, N-Queens, Sudoku solver, word search in a grid.

Interview trap: forgetting to undo the choice (unchoose step) corrupts the state for subsequent branches. Always restore state after recursion returns.

qa_pairs.question: What are the main Java Collections interfaces and their implementations?
qa_pairs.answer: List (ArrayList, LinkedList), Set (HashSet, TreeSet, LinkedHashSet), Map (HashMap, TreeMap, LinkedHashMap), Queue (ArrayDeque, PriorityQueue).
qa_pairs.explanation:
Java Collections Framework

List: ordered, allows duplicates. ArrayList — O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete middle. LinkedList — O(1) insert/delete at known position, O(n) access.

Set: no duplicates. HashSet — O(1) add/contains/remove, no order. TreeSet — O(log n), sorted order. LinkedHashSet — O(1), insertion order.

Map: key-value pairs, unique keys. HashMap — O(1) average, no order. TreeMap — O(log n), sorted by key. LinkedHashMap — O(1), insertion order.

Interview trap: HashMap allows one null key and multiple null values. TreeMap does NOT allow null keys (throws NullPointerException during comparison). HashSet is backed by a HashMap internally.

tasks.title: DSA: Array & String manipulation
tasks.description: Master Array & String manipulation concepts and problem-solving patterns for technical interviews.

qa_pairs.question: What is an array and how is it stored in memory?
qa_pairs.answer: An array is a contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type, accessible via index in O(1) time.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Array Memory Layout

Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations. Each element occupies the same amount of space, so the address of element[i] = base_address + (i × element_size). This enables O(1) random access.

In Python, lists are dynamic arrays — they over-allocate memory and resize when full (amortized O(1) append). In Java, arrays are fixed-size; use ArrayList for dynamic resizing.

Common operations: access O(1), search O(n), insert/delete at end O(1) amortized, insert/delete at middle O(n) due to shifting.

Interview trap: confusing array index out of bounds (runtime error) with a logic bug. Always validate index bounds before accessing.

qa_pairs.question: What is the Two Pointer technique and when do you use it?
qa_pairs.answer: Two pointers use two indices moving through an array to solve problems in O(n) instead of O(n²) nested loops.
qa_pairs.explanation:
Two Pointer Pattern

The two pointer technique places one pointer at the start and one at the end (or both at start moving at different speeds). By moving them based on conditions, you avoid nested loops.

Converging pointers: used for sorted array problems like 'find pair with target sum'. Move left pointer right if sum is too small, right pointer left if too large. O(n) time.

Fast-slow pointers: used for cycle detection in linked lists (Floyd's algorithm), finding the middle of a list, or detecting palindromes.

Interview trap: forgetting to handle duplicates or edge cases when both pointers meet. Always check the termination condition carefully.

qa_pairs.question: What is the Sliding Window technique?
qa_pairs.answer: Sliding window maintains a variable-size or fixed-size window over an array to compute results in O(n) instead of O(n²).
qa_pairs.explanation:
Sliding Window Pattern

A sliding window expands and contracts over a subarray to track a running result (sum, max, count of distinct elements). Instead of recomputing from scratch for each window, you add the new element and remove the old one.

Fixed window: window size k is constant. Compute the first window, then slide by adding the next element and removing the first. Used for 'maximum sum subarray of size k'.

Variable window: expand right pointer until a condition is violated, then shrink from the left. Used for 'longest substring without repeating characters'.

Interview trap: off-by-one errors in window boundaries. Use inclusive left and exclusive right, or both inclusive — be consistent.
